children.value: Project Leader & Instructor, Computational Behavioral Sciences, LS100, FAS, Harvard University
Below is a list of key Python terminologies every beginner should understand, along with brief explanations:
is the program that executes your Python code. Python is an
language, meaning you don’t compile your code into machine language before running it – instead, the interpreter reads and runs your code line by line
. When you installed Python, you obtained an interpreter (accessible via the
command or IDLE). If the interpreter encounters an error in your code, it will stop and display an error message (traceback) indicating what went wrong.

is simply a script or application written in Python – a set of instructions for the computer to execute. You can think of a program like a recipe: it contains steps (commands) that the computer follows to perform a task. Even a short Python script that prints a message is a program. Applications like web browsers or games are also programs (often composed of many files and modules). In general,
a program is a sequence of instructions
that tells the computer what to do
refers to the set of rules that define how Python code must be written. Python’s syntax is known for being clean and readable. One unique aspect is that
is meaningful in Python. Instead of using braces
to designate code blocks, Python uses indentation levels. For example, the lines inside a function or inside an
statement must be indented consistently. If the indentation is wrong or inconsistent, the interpreter will raise an
. Indentation thus determines the structure of code blocks
. This requirement makes Python code visually neat and helps enforce good formatting habits. Other syntax rules include using a colon
to start an indented block (e.g., after
conditions), and the use of line breaks to end statements (one statement per line, unless you use a backslash or other means to continue).
is text in the code meant for humans, not for execution. Comments are created by prefixing text with the
The Python interpreter ignores anything on a line after a
, so comments do not affect the program’s behavior. They are used to explain code or leave notes.
Comments are code lines that will not be executed
. Python also has a way to write multi-line comments or docstrings using triple quotes (
), commonly used to document functions or modules.
is a named storage location that holds a value. You can think of it as a container or a label for data. In Python, you create a variable by assigning a value to a name, for example:
is a variable name that refers to the value
. Variables make it possible to store and manipulate data in programs. Internally, a variable name points to an object representing the data in memory.
Basically, a variable is a place in computer memory with an assigned name, used to store data of different types (text, numbers, etc.)

. Unlike some languages, you don’t declare a type for a variable in Python – the type is inferred from whatever value you assign to it, and you can later change the value (and type) of the variable if needed.
defines what kind of value a piece of data is, which determines what you can do with it. Common Python data types include:
numbers with a decimal point or in exponential form (e.g.
sequence of characters used to represent text (e.g.
). Strings are enclosed in quotes. Python strings can be manipulated with many methods since they are a
type (you can think of them as a sequence of characters). For instance, you can find its length, slice it, or split it into parts. (Examples:
) and are used in control flow (if/while conditions). In Python,
are reserved keywords representing these two truth values.
a special type with a single value
, used to represent “no value” or the absence of data.
, meaning you don’t need to specify the type of a variable and a variable’s type can change when you assign a new value. You can always check the type of a value by using the
is a symbol (or sometimes a keyword) that tells the interpreter to perform a specific operation on one or more operands (values or variables). For example,
is an arithmetic operator that adds two numbers or concatenates strings. Python supports many types of operators:
for exponentiation) to perform mathematical calculations
) to compare two values, yielding a boolean result
, etc. to modify and assign) for setting variable values
) to check if two references point to the same object in memory.
) to test if a value is a member of a sequence or collection (e.g.
Using operators, you can form expressions like
. The operators follow standard precedence (e.g. multiplication comes before addition, unless parentheses are used to group).
are a fundamental control structure that let your program make decisions. An
statement executes a block of code only if a certain condition is true. In Python:
and a condition (an expression that evaluates to True or False). If the condition is True, the indented block under the
runs; if it’s False, the program can optionally check other conditions with
(else-if) or execute a default block under
Here, “Adult” will be printed because the condition
is False, so it goes to the
. Conditions often use comparison operators (==, >, <, etc.) and logical operators (
after the condition and indentation to mark the block of code that belongs to the
. Proper indentation is crucial, as mentioned earlier, to ensure the
. If statements allow programs to branch and perform different actions based on input or state.
allow you to execute a block of code repeatedly. Python has two main loop types:
used to iterate over elements of a sequence (like a list, tuple, set, string, or range of numbers). In Python, a
. It will assign each element of the collection to
and execute the indented block for each element
This will print each fruit name in the list. A
loop is ideal when you know in advance how many elements (or iterations) you need to go through.
used to repeat a block as long as a condition remains true. It looks like
. The loop will keep running, checking the condition before each iteration, and it will stop once the condition becomes false
This will print 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 each on a new line, then stop when count is 0 (since the condition
is no longer true). You must ensure that the condition will eventually become false, otherwise a while loop can run forever.
Both loop types use indentation to define the loop body. You can use the keyword
to exit a loop early, or
to skip to the next iteration. In general,
are common when iterating over known collections or ranges, and

are useful when the repetition should continue until a condition changes (which might depend on user input or some calculation). Loops are essential for tasks like iterating through data, repeating an operation multiple times, or searching through collections
is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. You can define your own functions using the
keyword, giving the function a name and optional parameters. For example:
would print “Hello, Alice”. Functions allow you to organize code into logical pieces and avoid repetition – you write the code once and call it whenever needed.
A function is a reusable block of code that performs a certain action (or actions). Functions are used to accomplish tasks, especially those that need to be done repeatedly, with different inputs
, etc.), and you can import functions from modules. When a function needs to produce a result, it can
a value, which then can be used by the caller. If a function doesn’t explicitly return anything, it returns
is one of Python’s most basic and frequently used built-in functions. It outputs messages to the screen (or more technically, to the standard output). For example,
will display that text. You can print variables and other data types as well.
is very handy for showing results or for debugging by inspecting variable values.
Every Python developer learns to use
early, as it allows us to display the result of code or messages for the user
adds a newline at the end of what it prints, so each call prints on a new line (this can be changed with an optional parameter).
is the counterpart to print – it allows you to
, the program will pause and wait for the user to type something and press Enter. The text the user enters is then returned as a string. You can optionally pass a string to
If the user types “21” and hits enter, then
. You might then convert this to an integer with
function always returns the user input as a string (even if they typed digits)
is one way to make programs interactive and dynamic.

refers to an instance of a data type that encapsulates both data and functionality. Practically everything in Python is an object – integers, strings, lists, functions, even modules are objects. Each object has a type (or class) that defines what it is and what it can do. For example, the number
, and it has certain behaviors (like it can be added or subtracted). A list like
. If you define a class (blueprint) for something, the actual realized entities are objects.
In short, an object is a concrete instance of some class or data type, bundling together data and operations
. For a real-world analogy, if you have a class
(concept), then a specific car (like your dad’s red Toyota) is an object (instance) of that class.

is like a blueprint or template for creating objects. It defines a new data type by specifying what attributes (data) and methods (functions) its objects will have. You can define a class in Python using the
. From this class, you can create objects (instances) like
A class can be thought of as a blueprint for objects – it defines the structure and behavior (via attributes and methods) that the objects of the class will have
. Python has many built-in classes (for example,

are classes), and you can also create your own to model more complex data. One advantage of using classes is that you can create multiple objects from the same blueprint without repeating code, and you can leverage concepts like inheritance (one class inheriting attributes/methods from another). For beginners, it’s enough to grasp that classes = blueprints, objects = actual instances.
is a piece of data stored in an object (or class). If we think in terms of Python code, attributes are the
variables/properties that belong to an object or class
a piece of data specific to an object (instance). In the
object has its own values for these.
a variable that is defined at the class level (not in any method) and is shared by all instances of the class.
(see below) are sometimes called “behavioral attributes” or “procedural attributes” of a class.
Data attributes define what an object needs to have (state), while methods define how to interact with the object (behavior)
For example, if you have an object
, that method is also considered an attribute of the class (a function attribute). In Python, you access attributes with the dot notation: e.g.
. Trying to access an attribute that doesn’t exist will result in an
. Understanding attributes is important when dealing with objects and classes, as it helps organize data and functionality together.
is another name for an object in the context of a class. We say an object is an instance of a particular class. For example, using the
. You can create multiple instances (objects) from one class, each with their own attribute values.
An instance of a class is an individual object that possesses the structure and behavior defined by that class

In conversation, “instance” and “object” are often used interchangeably. The word instance emphasizes the relationship to its class (like “this is an instance of class Car”). Checking an object’s type with
will show you its class, and
returns True if the object is an instance of that class (or a subclass of it).
is a function that is defined inside a class and is meant to be called on objects of that class. Methods typically operate on the object’s data. For example, in a list object

method of the list, which adds a new element. Methods are described as the behavior of an object. The only difference between a method and a regular function is that a method is tied to an object (the first parameter of a method is usually
Methods are similar to functions, but they belong to a particular class and are used to interact with class instances
is a method that returns an uppercase version of a string. In our
is a method; you would call

, which would perhaps reduce fuel or print a message. Understanding methods is important because much of Python’s functionality comes from calling methods on objects (e.g. list methods to manipulate lists, dict methods to handle dictionaries, etc.).

is a built-in data structure in Python that holds an ordered collection of items. Lists are very flexible – they can contain elements of any type, and you can add, remove, or change items. Lists are created using square brackets, e.g.
. The items in a list have a defined order (index starting from 0 for the first item) and the list can be modified (it’s
). You can access elements by index (
In summary, a list is a data type that can store a sequence of values, accessible by an index (position)
Lists are one of the most commonly used data structures for storing collections of items.
is another built-in sequence type, quite similar to a list, but with one key difference: tuples are
. Once a tuple is created, its contents cannot be changed (you can’t add, remove, or modify items in place). Tuples are defined using parentheses, for example
. They support indexing and slicing like lists (

). Because they are immutable, tuples are useful for representing fixed collections of items (such as a pair of coordinates, or a database record that shouldn’t change). Tuples can also be used as keys in dictionaries (since immutability makes them hashable), whereas lists cannot.
In essence, a tuple is a sequence of objects, very much like a list, but defined and fixed upon creation
If you need an ordered collection that you won’t need to modify, a tuple is a good choice.

(dict) is a built-in Python data structure that stores a collection of key–value pairs. It’s also sometimes called a hash map or associative array in other languages. You create a dictionary with curly braces
are their respective values. You retrieve or set values by key, e.g.

collections (as of Python 3.7+, insertion order is preserved, but conceptually you don’t rely on order) and are mutable (you can add, remove, or change entries). Keys in a dict must be unique and immutable (numbers, strings, tuples, etc. can be keys; lists cannot).
A Python dictionary stores an unordered collection of data values, where each element has a key and a value
They are very powerful for representing structured data and performing fast lookups by key (like a word-to-definition mapping, or a username-to-user info mapping). For instance, you could have
to map items to their prices.
is a built-in data type that represents an

. Sets are like mathematical sets – they don’t allow duplicates and the order of elements is not preserved. You create a set using curly braces with comma-separated values (similar to dict literal but just values, no colons), e.g.
constructor to create a set from another iterable (like a list). Key features of sets:
Fast membership testing: you can check if an item is in a set with
Because they only allow unique items, sets are useful for removing duplicates from a collection.
They support operations like union, intersection, difference (via methods or operators like
In Python, a set is an unordered collection of distinct (unique) immutable objects
. Sets themselves are mutable (you can add or remove elements), but because they are unordered, you cannot access elements by index. There is also a special type called a
which is an immutable set. Beginners commonly use sets when they need to gather unique items or perform mathematical set operations.
We touched on strings earlier as a data type, but to elaborate: a
in Python is a sequence of characters used to represent text. Strings are created by enclosing characters in quotes, either single
(you cannot change a string in place; operations that appear to modify a string actually create a new string). Python provides many helpful operations for strings:
to get the number of characters.
you can access characters by index (
. Indices start at 0, and negative indices count from the end (
There are many built-in methods, e.g.
, etc., to transform or search within the string. For example,
Strings are fundamental for input/output, representing messages, file paths, data read from files, etc. Even though they are immutable, operations are designed to be efficient and convenient.

(or library or module – the terms are related, see below) is a collection of pre-written code that you can include in your projects to add functionality. The Python Standard Library is the collection of packages/modules included with Python by default (for tasks like math, file I/O, data structures, etc.). Additionally, the Python community has tens of thousands of third-party packages available via the Python Package Index (PyPI). A
in the filesystem sense is a directory containing one or more Python modules (usually identified by an
colloquially, we often mean any installable library or tool. Examples include
Python offers a rich selection of packages; any developer can create a package and publish it on PyPI for others to install
– instead of writing something from scratch, you can install a package that provides the functionality you need.
is a single Python file (with a

extension) that can be imported, or a component of a larger package. When you import a module, you’re essentially loading that file and making its functions/classes available in your code. For example, Python’s standard library has a module called
in the library), and you can do
etc. If you have a file

In terms of relationship: a package may contain multiple modules (files), whereas a module is a single file. Often, you will only need to import a specific module from a package rather than the whole package at once
For instance, a large package might have submodules and you could do
. But if you’re not creating your own packages yet, you can think of modules simply as “libraries” or files you import.
is the standard package installer for Python. It’s the tool you use to
from the Python Package Index (and other repositories). For example, if you want to install the requests library, you would run
in your command prompt. Pip will download the package and its dependencies and install them into your Python environment.
In other words, pip is the package installer for Python – you can use pip to install packages from the Python Package Index (PyPI) and other indexes
Pip typically comes bundled with Python installers. To use it, open a terminal and type
. You can also use it to upgrade packages (
). Knowing how to use pip is essential for leveraging Python’s vast ecosystem of external libraries. In environments like Anaconda, you might use
for packages available through conda, but pip can still be used there as well.
